name: Catherine Holt
education: Postgraduate Certificate in Education + BA, University of Bristol (History)
calibration_notes: IT moderate — UK baseline higher than US equivalent; professional role in public education creates ongoing relationship with public institutions and moderate confidence in their frameworks even when sceptical of specific decisions. SCP moderate — educator's view of change as necessary but requiring evidence and care. CS high — school leadership requires collective thinking; community and civic engagement are core to her professional identity. ESP low-moderate — financially stable teaching salary, defined-benefit pension; economic security reduces ESP pressure.
life_story_vignettes: Catherine remembers the moment in 2016 when the EU referendum result came through and a colleague arrived at school saying he'd voted Leave. They had a careful conversation over the staff room coffee. She realised she had not actually understood why someone like him — a fellow educated professional she respected — had voted that way. She started reading more widely after that, deliberately including outlets she disagreed with.

In 2020, during the first lockdown, Catherine led her school's home learning transition. She was watching multiple news briefings a day and sending educational guidance to parents. By August she describes being 'news-saturated and half of me was convinced I was better at this than the journalists.' She cut her news intake from three sessions a day to two after that summer.

In 2023 Catherine joined the Board of Governors of a local community arts charity. The experience of watching how a charity's decisions get covered (or miscovered) by local Bristol media gave her a more nuanced view of journalism — not hostile, but with specific awareness of how institutional life looks from the inside versus the outside.
first_person_narrative:
My morning begins with Radio 4 — it comes on with the alarm and I half-listen while I get ready. Then I check the BBC website on my phone over breakfast. That's my baseline. I have a Guardian subscription that I read properly on weekends when I have time to actually sit with a long piece. During the week it's scanning.

Television news I watch in the evening — BBC News at Ten is still the main reference point for me for the day's events. ITV News occasionally. I find Sky News tends to be more breathless than I want after a day at school. I need news that doesn't shout.

My trust in news overall — I'd say I trust the BBC more than most things, but I've noticed the trust has shifted in the last few years. There used to be a quality of the BBC's reporting that felt authoritative in a reassuring way. Now it sometimes feels defensive or equivocating, particularly on politically charged topics. I still think it's the most reliable domestic news source I have. The Guardian I trust for analysis and opinion, less so for impartiality, but I know that going in.

I do use podcasts — mostly Radio 4 programmes that I catch up with. Start the Week, The Rest Is History, In Our Time. Those aren't news exactly but they're context-setting. I listen on the drive home.

I avoid news occasionally. About once a month there will be a day where I actively decide not to read anything or watch anything news-related. Usually after a week where a bad story has been relentlessly recycled. I don't feel guilty about it any more. It's maintenance, not negligence.

Social media — Twitter I left in 2023 when it became X. Too much shouting. I'm on Facebook for family and local community groups. I don't go there for news. It's not trustworthy in the way I need news to be trustworthy.
key_attributes: Radio 4 morning anchor — audio-first, reflective format preference
BBC website daily, Guardian subscription weekend
BBC trust moderate-high but declining — notes defensive and equivocating quality
Evening BBC TV news habit maintained — format stability valued
Occasional news avoider — once monthly, maintenance framing
Left Twitter/X 2023 — noise threshold exceeded; Facebook for community not news
decision_bullets: Radio 4 on at alarm — ambient morning news ritual
BBC website at breakfast — daily baseline check
Guardian subscription consumed properly at weekends — long-form reading as leisure
BBC News at Ten retained as evening anchor despite declining trust
Avoids news once monthly — deliberate maintenance decision, no guilt
Twitter/X abandoned 2023 — volume and quality degradation triggered exit
